segments.s146-g49: ز بس is a causal/quantifying idiom meaning 'from so much' / 'because of so many' (here: 'there were so many...'). The following coordinated nouns خیمه و مرد و پرده‌سرای form a simple enumerated list 'tents and men and curtained-pavilions'; the conjunction و links the items without additional inflection. This whole noun phrase is the quantity/topic that the verb نماند (in the next segment) comments on.
segments.s146-g50: نماند is the negated past/perfective verb 'did not remain / was not left' (ن- prefix negates the verb). ایچ is an archaic/poetic form of هیچ ('nothing/anything') and here is the direct negated element: 'nothing remained'. The prepositional phrases بر دشت و بر کوه ('on the plain and on the mountain') specify the location of the lack of place; جای ('place') names the thing that did not remain (= 'no place remained'). Word order is somewhat poetic/inverted (verb then negated object then locative phrases) but yields the natural English: 'no place remained on the plain or on the mountain.'
segments.s146-g9: Noun phrases 'سراپرده و خیمه' (inner curtains / tents) come before the verb 'زد' (lit. 'struck'), a common classical Persian idiom meaning 'pitched' (as in خیمه زدن). 'بر دو میل' is a prepositional phrase with 'بر' ('on/upon') governing its measure 'دو میل' ('two masts/poles'). Word order is subject/object(s) → verb → prepositional phrase, typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s146-g10: 'بپوشید' is a past-tense (perfect) verb here meaning 'clothed/covered' (literally 'he/she/it clothed'). The following noun 'گیتی' is the entity being clothed (object/patient). The repeated preposition 'به' functions instrumentally ('with/by'), so 'به نعل و به پیل' means 'with horseshoes and with elephants' (i.e. by horses' hoofs and elephants' feet). This construction yields a pictorial statement: the earth was covered by trampling horses and elephants.
segments.s146-g11: Simple clause: subject 'هوا' (the air/sky) + predicate adjective 'نیلگون گشت' (became indigo). The coordinating 'و' links the next nominal phrase 'کوه آبنوس'—a condensed apposition meaning 'the mountain, (black) as ebony'—creating a parallel two-part description.
segments.s146-g12: 'بجوشید' is past tense 'boiled/seethed' with subject 'دریا' (the sea). The preposition 'ز' (poetic 'from/because of') introduces the cause: 'ز آواز کوس' = 'from the sound/call of the war-drum'. The clause thus states cause-and-effect: the drum's sound made the sea 'boil' (seethe/agitated).
segments.s146-g13: 'همی' is a habitual/continuous adverb ('still/kept on') modifying the past verb 'رفت' ('went/was going'). The reduplicated 'منزل به منزل' is an idiom meaning 'from stage to stage / from place to place'; 'جهان' locates the movement 'throughout the world'. The word order (adverb → verb → path phrase → scope) is typical of classical narrative.
segments.s146-g14: 'شده' functions impersonally/predicatively as 'it became / it happened', followed by the comparative 'چون' ('as/like') introducing 'شب و روز' (night and day). 'گشته' is a perfect participle ('having become / turned') modifying 'نهان' ('hidden'), so the clause literally reads 'it became as if night and day had become hidden'—a simile expressing sudden concealment or darkness.
segments.s146-g15: 'درخشیدن' is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the shining/gleaming' and is used here predicatively with the following coordinated nominatives 'خشت و ژوپین' (bricks and padded jackets/armor). 'ز گرد' means 'from the dust/around'—the preposition 'ز' ('from') plus 'گرد' in the sense 'dust'—so the phrase describes the glittering emerging through dust.
segments.s146-g16: 'چو' introduces a simile 'like/as', so 'چو آتش' = 'like fire'. 'پس پرده' is a bound phrase meaning 'behind the curtain' ('پس' here = 'after/behind'), and 'لاجورد' characterizes that curtain as lapis-lazuli (deep blue). The line thus compares the gleam to fire seen behind a blue curtain.
segments.s146-g41: This is a fronted temporal/agential phrase: کنون (now) + من (I) gives the speaker and time; به بخت is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by fate' (به + بخت). رد افراسیاب uses رد as a nominal/verbal noun 'driving away / putting to flight' with the proper name افراسیاب as its object; no finite verb appears here because the main verb follows in the next segment.
segments.s146-g42: کنم is the first-person singular present/past-form verb 'I will do / I make' (here future/intended action). دشت را is the definite object marked by را. همچو ... آب is a simile: همچو 'like, as' + دریای آب 'a sea of water' describing what the speaker will make the plain into; the verb کنم applies back to the action implied in the previous segment as well.
segments.s146-g43: به تنگی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/narrowness / distress' (به + تنگی). نداد is the negative past 'did not give' (here 'did not yield/submit'). ایچ 'not at all' intensifies the negation. The nominal phrase سهراب دل ('Sohrab [the] heart') places the person and 'heart' together as the subject; poetic word order places دل after the name.
segments.s146-g44: فرود آمد is a compound meaning 'came down / sank' (فرود as noun/verb element + آمد). از باره is a causal/instrumental phrase 'from/under the burden' (باره here = load/burden), and شاداب دل 'the lively/vigorous heart' names what sank; word order places the descriptive دل after شاداب.
segments.s146-g45: یکی is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one' functioning as the subject. جام is the object 'a cup', and می‌خواست is the past/habitual verb 'wanted' (imperfect sense). از می‌گسار is a source phrase 'from the cupbearer' (از + می‌گسار). The phrase follows normal subject–object–verb order with the source marked after the verb.
segments.s146-g46: نکرد is the negated past 'did not do / did not cause' (here functioning as 'was not' or 'did not become'). ایچ 'not at all' intensifies the negation. رنجه here is a participial/adjectival use 'distressed / troubled', and دل is the patient/subject 'the heart'. از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the battle' specifying the source of trouble that did not affect the heart.
segments.s146-g47: وزانسو is a contracted locative phrase meaning 'from that side / on that side' (poetic contraction of وز آن سو); it sets the spatial scene. سراپرده شهریار is a genitival phrase 'the king's main tent / pavilion' where سراپرده ('inner tent / main curtain') is modified by شهریار; poetic order puts modifier after the head.
segments.s146-g48: کشیدند is the past plural 'they stretched / they drew (up)' (here 'set up / spread out' tents). بر دشت is the locative 'on the plain' (بر = on/over, دشت = plain). پیش حصار means 'in front of the fortress' (پیش = before/in front of, حصار = fortress), completing the place where the tents were pitched.
segments.s146-g25: خروشی بلند آمد از دیدگاه is built like: noun + adjective (خروشی بلند, 'a loud cry'), then the past verb آمد ('came/rose'). از دیدگاه is a prepositional phrase (from/out of the lookout/viewpoint) modifying the source of the sound. The word order (noun‑phrase + verb + prepositional phrase) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s146-g26: به سهراب گفتند کامد سپاه: به سهراب ('to Sohrab') is the indirect object marked by the preposition به, گفتند is the past plural 'they said' (here introducing reported speech), and کامد سپاه is the reported clause 'the army comes/is coming' using the archaic/poetic form کامد (3rd person). In Persian the dative phrase often precedes گفتند as shown here.
segments.s146-g27: چو 'when' introduces a temporal subordinate clause: چو سهراب زان دیده آوا شنید = 'when Sohrab heard that sound from that lookout.' زان ('from that') refers back to the previously mentioned watch/post; دیده here functions as 'lookout/eye' and شنید is the past tense 'he heard.'
segments.s146-g28: به باره بیامد سپه بنگرید is a sequence of actions: به باره ('to the spot/scene') + بیامد (past 'he came'), then سپه بنگرید ('he gazed at the host/army'). Note the poetic ordering: motion verb (بیامد) followed by the verb of perception (بنگرید) that takes سپه as its object.
segments.s146-g29: به انگشت لشکر به هومان نمود: به انگشت here functions instrumentally ('with a finger' — poetic use of به rather than modern با), لشکر is the thing pointed at, به هومان is the indirect object 'to/for Houman', and نمود is the past form meaning 'showed/pointed out.' The clause orders instrument → object → recipient → verb, acceptable in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s146-g30: سپاهی که آن را کرانه نبود is a relative clause modifying سپاهی: که introduces the relative 'which/that', آن را ('it' with object marker for emphasis) is the referent, کرانه نبود = 'had no edge/shore' i.e. 'had no limit' (نبود is the past negative/existential 'there was not'). Together: 'an army that had no boundary.'
segments.s146-g31: چو هومان ز دور آن سپه را بدید: چو ('when') introduces the temporal clause; ز دور ('from afar') gives location/distance; آن سپه را is the definite object ('that army' with object marker را), and بدید is the past tense 'he saw.' The prepositional phrase z‑dour appears between subject and object as is common in Persian.
segments.s146-g32: دلش گشت پربیم و دم درکشید: دلش ('his heart') + گشت ('became') + پربیم ('full of fear') expresses an internal change of state. و دم درکشید links the clause with 'and he drew in his breath' — درکشید here means 'drew in' (breath), a physical reaction to fear. Note the possessive clitic ش on دل: 'his heart.'
segments.s146-g33: This is a free word order typical of epic Persian: به هومان (to Homan) + چنین گفت (said thus) + سهراب گرد (Sohrab, epithet). گفت is the past tense verb 'said'; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گرد here follows the name as an epithet. The whole segment means 'Sohrab the brave said this to Homan.'
segments.s146-g34: که introduces a reported content clause ('that ...'). اندیشه از دل is a noun phrase 'thought from the heart' (از = 'from'). بباید is the modal present 'must/ought' (literally 'it should be'), followed by سترد, an older poetic third‑person present/subjunctive meaning 'remove/banish' (here 'drive out' or 'erase'). Together بباید سترد = 'must be driven away'.
segments.s146-g35: نبینی is the negative of بینی (2nd‑person present) used here as a general statement 'you will not see / you will not find'. تو 'you' is emphatic. زین لشکر is a poetic contraction (ز + این) meaning 'of/from this army' or 'in this army'; بیکران modifies لشکر as 'boundless' or 'countless'.
segments.s146-g36: یکی begins the noun phrase 'one/a single', followed by مرد جنگی 'a warlike man / a warrior'. و joins گرزی گران 'and a heavy mace' (گرزی = 'mace', گران = 'heavy' / 'weighty'). The phrase enumerates the two kinds of things you will not find in the host.
segments.s146-g37: که heads a relative clause modifying 'one' from the previous line: 'who ...'. پیش من آید is 'comes before me / appears in my presence' (پیش meaning 'before, in the presence of', آید = 'comes'). به آوردگاه = 'to the battlefield' (به + آوردگاه).
segments.s146-g38: گر here works like 'even if' or 'though'; ایدون is a poetic/older form 'if he comes' or 'should come'. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. یاری دهد is present/subjunctive 'gives aid' (دهد = 'gives'). هور و ماه ('sun and moon' / metaphorical stellar helpers) are the agents of help in the clause; together the clause means 'even if the sun and moon were to give aid'.
segments.s146-g39: سلیح‌ست is an archaic/poetic copula phrase meaning 'is armed' or 'armed are (they)'; بسیار here amplifies it 'very many / plentifully'. و links the next clause; مردم بسی means 'the people are many/abundant' (بسی is an older adverb meaning 'much / many'). The segment lists the army's armament and the crowd's size.
segments.s146-g40: سرافراز نامی is a nominal phrase 'renowned and exalted' (سرافراز = 'lofty/honored', نامی = 'famous'). ندانم is the first‑person present 'I do not know' (negative of دانم). کسی is indefinite 'anyone' — the speaker claims he knows no one who is both famous and exalted.
segments.s146-g17: ز بس is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'because of so much' or 'from so many'; بس here functions as an intensifier ('so many/so much'). گونه‌گونه is a reduplicated adjective meaning 'various' and follows the normal Persian modifier-before-noun order (adjective precedes the noun it qualifies in poetry). سنان و درفش are coordinated nouns ('spears and standards') linked by و; the whole phrase is an ablative/causal phrase describing cause or abundance.
segments.s146-g18: This is a noun phrase listing items: سپرهای زرین = 'golden shields' (سپر + plural marker های + adjective زرین) and زرینه کفش = 'gilded/golden shoes' (زرینه an adjectival form modifying کفش). The conjunction و links the two coordinated noun phrases; زرینه is a slightly more ornamental adjective than زرین (poetic variation).
segments.s146-g19: تو گفتی که is the reporting clause 'you said that' (که introducing the subordinate clause). ابری is an indefinite/nominative noun 'a cloud' and به رنگ آبنوس is a prepositional/qualifying phrase 'of the color of ebony' (به + رنگ + noun) describing the cloud's color. Word order is typical: reporter ('you said') + که + descriptive clause.
segments.s146-g20: This is two consecutive past actions: برآمد is past tense 'arose' (intransitive), then ببارید 'it rained' (past of باریدن). زو here is a poetic contraction of از او ('from it/him') meaning 'from it' (the cloud); سندروس names the thing that rained (sandalwood/resin). The sequence shows the cloud's rising followed by an extraordinary rain from it.
segments.s146-g21: جهان را is the topical/definite object marked phrase 'as for the world' (را marking the theme). شب و روز 'night and day' are coordinated nouns; پیدا نبود is the past negative impersonally meaning 'were not visible' or 'could not be seen' (پیدا 'visible' + نبود 'was not'). Word order places the topic first, then the negated predicate.
segments.s146-g22: تو گفتی again introduces reported speech 'you said'; سپهر و ثریا نبود is another predication: 'the sky and the Pleiades were not' (نبود = 'were not' in past). The coordination with و links two celestial terms being asserted as absent.
segments.s146-g23: ازینسان بشد is a formulaic narrative phrase 'thus it came to pass' (ازینسان 'thus' + بشد, here past 'it became/it happened'). تا introduces a limit/extent 'until', and در دژ رسید is a past-tense clause 'it reached the fortress' (در = 'in/into', دژ 'fortress', رسید 'arrived/reached'). The clause sequence indicates the event continued up to the fortress.
segments.s146-g24: بشد here repeats the past 'it became/it happened' and takes a following nominal phrase as its subject/patient: خاک و سنگ 'earth and stone'. از جهان ناپدید literally 'from the world [they] disappeared' (از = 'from', جهان 'world', ناپدید 'vanished'). The verb is implicit for the plural subject but understood from بشد/ناپدید combination.
segments.s146-g1: دگر روز is a temporal phrase 'the next/another day' placed before the verb; فرمود is a past-tense honorific verb 'he said/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command ('that' / 'so that'), and the names گیو و طوس are the coordinated subjects of that clause (گیو and طوس). Word order is: time — main verb — subordinating marker — subject(s) of subordinate clause.
segments.s146-g2: ببستند is the verb of the subordinate clause (3rd person plural) meaning 'they tied/fastened' (classical narrative form). شبگیر functions as the thing being fastened (a night-cover/guard), followed by the preposition بر 'on/upon' and the compound noun پیل کوس where پیل = 'elephant' and کوس = 'war-drum' (object phrase 'upon the elephant's drum'). The verb agrees with the plural subject گیو و طوس from the previous segment.
segments.s146-g3: در گنج بگشاد is a short clause where در گنج 'in/into the treasury' is the locative object and بگشاد is a past verb meaning 'opened' (literally 'caused to open'). و links to the next action; روزی بداد is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'he gave sustenance/provisions' (روزی = provisions/sustenance, بداد = 'he gave'), with روزی as the direct object and بداد the past verb.
segments.s146-g4: سپه برنشاند is another past action: سپه 'the army/host' is the object and برنشاند is a causative/past verb meaning 'he set forth/marshaled' or 'caused to take position'. و connects the next clause; بنه برنهاد has بنه (a small bundle/baggage) as direct object and برنهاد 'placed on/laid out' as the past verb — here it means he had the baggage placed/loaded.
segments.s146-g5: سپردار و جوشنوران is a coordinated noun phrase: سپردار 'the commander' followed by و and جوشنوران (armored/mail-bearers). The phrase صد هزار is a numeric quantifier 'a hundred thousand' that applies to the group mentioned. This is a nominal headline giving who was present/arrayed.
segments.s146-g6: شمرده is a past participial/relational form 'numbered/count(ed)' modifying or describing how they stand with respect to the army; به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'among/in the host' (به + لشکر) indicating membership; گه is an adverb 'occasionally/at times' modifying آمد 'came', and سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman' that is the subject of آمد. The clause thus reads 'counted among the host, at times a horseman came.'
segments.s146-g7: یکی لشکر آمد is 'one/one of the armies came' where یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain', لشکر is 'host/army', and آمد is the past verb 'came'. ز پهلو is a prepositional phrase 'from the flank' (ز = from, پهلو = flank/side), and به دشت is the directional phrase 'to the plain' (به + دشت). Word order: subject (one host) — verb (came) — origin phrase — direction phrase.
segments.s146-g8: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/from which'; از گرد ایشان is 'from around them' (از = from, گرد = around/about or 'dust' in context, ایشان = they/them). هوا تیره گشت is a copular/result clause: هوا 'the air/atmosphere' as subject, تیره 'dark' as predicate adjective, گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' so the clause means 'and the air became dark from their dust.'

sentences.s146-s25: There were so many tents, men, and curtained pavilions that no place remained on the plain or on the mountain.
sentences.s146-s5: They pitched inner curtains and tents on two masts; the earth was covered by horses' hoofs and by elephants' feet.
sentences.s146-s6: The air turned indigo and the mountain became ebony; the sea boiled from the sound of the drum.
sentences.s146-s7: He kept going from place to place across the world; it was as though night and day had been made hidden.
sentences.s146-s8: Bricks and coats of mail gleamed from the dust like fire behind a lapis-blue curtain.
sentences.s146-s21: Now I will, by fate, put Afrasiab to flight and make the plain like a sea of water.
sentences.s146-s22: Sohrab's heart did not at all yield to hardship; the buoyant heart sank beneath the burden.
sentences.s146-s23: Someone wanted a cup from the cupbearer; the heart was not at all troubled by the battle.
sentences.s146-s24: From that side they stretched the king's pavilion (tents) across the plain before the fortress.
sentences.s146-s13: A loud cry rose from the lookout; they told Sohrab, 'the army is coming.'
sentences.s146-s14: When Sohrab heard that sound from the lookout, he came toward the spot and looked at the army.
sentences.s146-s15: With a finger he pointed out the army to Houman; it was an army that had no boundary.
sentences.s146-s16: When Houman saw that host from afar, his heart was filled with fear and he drew in his breath.
sentences.s146-s17: Sohrab the mighty said this to Homan: that one must drive the thought out of the heart.
sentences.s146-s18: You will not find in this boundless host a single fighting man and a heavy mace.
sentences.s146-s19: who would come before me to the battlefield, even if the sun and moon were to give aid.
sentences.s146-s20: They are well armed and the people are numerous; I do not know anyone famed and exalted.
sentences.s146-s9: Because there were so many varied spears and standards, golden shields and gilded shoes.
sentences.s146-s10: You said that a cloud, the color of ebony, rose; it rained from it sandalwood.
sentences.s146-s11: Day and night could not be seen in the world; you said the sky and the Pleiades were not there.
sentences.s146-s12: Thus it happened until it reached the fortress; earth and rock became vanished from the world.
sentences.s146-s1: The next day he commanded that Giv and Tus fasten night-guards upon the war-elephant's drum.
sentences.s146-s2: He opened the treasure and gave out provisions; he marshaled the host and set the baggage in place.
sentences.s146-s3: A commander and the mail-clad (warriors), a hundred thousand counted in the host; at times a horseman would appear.
sentences.s146-s4: One force came from the flank onto the plain, and from around them the air grew dark.
